subsystem: libgadu (Gadu-Gadu instant-messaging protocol client library). This is the outbound client function gg_change_status_descr(), which sets the local user's presence status and free-text description and serialises them into a GG_NEW_STATUS80 packet sent to the GG server. It builds a fixed on-wire header struct gg_new_status80 followed by a variable-length description payload.
provenance: Identified by the unmistakable libgadu naming and API surface: gg_session, gg_new_status80, gg_fix32() (host<->little-endian fixups), gg_send_packet(), gg_encoding_convert(), gg_debug_session(), and the GG_* constants (GG_STATE_CONNECTED, GG_ENCODING_UTF8/CP1250, GG_PROTOCOL_110, GG_NEW_STATUS80, GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE, GG_S_NA). The Polish comments ("pamiętać o tym, żeby nie ucinać w środku znaku utf-8", "dodaj \0 na końcu") confirm the libgadu/Gadu-Gadu origin. Sample tag in the banner: primevul_c_train_3405_vuln.
trust_boundary: This is OUTBOUND code: the direction of untrusted flow is unusual. The inputs status and descr are supplied by the LOCAL application/user of libgadu, so they are only weakly adversarial (a hostile local caller, or an over-long/mis-encoded description). The more interesting boundary is the REVERSE one: whatever bytes this function serialises (the struct p and the descr payload) are transmitted to the remote GG server. Any uninitialised or over-length bytes placed into the packet therefore cross a process trust boundary and are disclosed to a remote party. sess->state, sess->encoding, sess->status_flags and sess->protocol_version are session state; sess itself may be NULL.

what_the_code_does.explanation: The entry point takes the session handle sess, an integer status code, and a C string descr (the status description text). It declares p, a stack-allocated on-wire packet header of type struct gg_new_status80 (the fixed part of the GG_NEW_STATUS80 message). p is NOT zero-initialised here. gen_descr is a scratch pointer that will hold a heap buffer if an encoding conversion is performed (initialised NULL so the later free() is safe on all paths). descr_len is a signed int that will hold the byte length of the description. res captures the send result. The function returns int (0/positive on success, -1 on error with errno set).

what_the_code_does.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
what_the_code_does.explanation: A debug trace logging the arguments. Note it dereferences/uses sess and formats descr with %s BEFORE the sess NULL-check and before descr is proven non-NULL. gg_debug_session typically tolerates a NULL session, and printf %s on a NULL pointer is undefined but many libc implementations print "(null)"; nonetheless this call runs first.

Two precondition gates. First it rejects a NULL session with EFAULT. Then it requires the session to be in GG_STATE_CONNECTED, otherwise ENOTCONN. These ensure the packet is only built and sent on an established connection and that sess is a valid pointer for the rest of the function.

Records the caller-requested status into the session structure. This is a plain scalar assignment with no validation of status against the set of legal GG status enum values; the raw integer is stored and later serialised.

If a description is supplied and the session is not already UTF-8, the text is transcoded from CP1250 to UTF-8. gg_encoding_convert allocates a new heap buffer; both descr and gen_descr are pointed at it so descr now aliases the converted copy and gen_descr owns it for later free(). The (-1, -1) length arguments tell the converter to treat the input as NUL-terminated and produce a NUL-terminated output of unbounded length. On allocation/conversion failure it returns NULL and the function bails with -1 (no leak, gen_descr is NULL).

Computes the payload length. If descr is present, descr_len = strlen(descr) (a size_t result stored into a signed int). It is then clamped to GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE, a protocol-defined ceiling on the description field. The XXX comment ("remember not to cut in the middle of a utf-8 character") is an author-acknowledged known defect: the byte-count clamp can split a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence. If descr is NULL, it is replaced with the empty string literal "" and descr_len stays 0 so a well-formed empty description is sent.

Fills the fixed header struct p. gg_fix32() byte-swaps each 32-bit field into the GG wire byte order (little-endian). p.status carries the status code, p.flags the session status flags, and p.description_size the clamped description length (the pre-increment value). Only these three members are assigned; any other members or padding bytes of p remain whatever was on the stack.

For protocol 11.0 and newer, the flags field is overwritten with the constant 0x00000014, and descr_len is incremented by one so the trailing NUL terminator of the description is included in the transmitted payload ("add \0 at the end"). Crucially description_size at line 43 was already fixed to the PRE-increment length, so on GG_PROTOCOL_110 the declared size and the transmitted byte count differ by one (the NUL), which is the intended proto-11 framing.

what_the_code_does.code:
res = gg_send_packet(sess, GG_NEW_STATUS80, 
			&p, sizeof(p), 
			descr, descr_len,
			NULL);
what_the_code_does.explanation: Serialises and queues the packet. gg_send_packet is a variadic gather-writer: it takes (session, packet type, then pairs of {pointer, length} terminated by NULL). Here it sends the fixed header &p of length sizeof(p) followed by the description buffer descr of length descr_len. The full struct p including any uninitialised bytes is transmitted, and descr_len bytes are read from the descr buffer.

Releases the heap buffer allocated by gg_encoding_convert. If no conversion happened, gen_descr is still NULL and free(NULL) is a no-op, so this is safe on every path. Note descr may still alias this freed buffer, but it is not used again after the send.

If the requested status is a "not available"/offline status (GG_S_NA macro), the session is moved to the disconnecting state with a disconnect timeout so the client tears down after the status is delivered. Finally the send result res is returned to the caller.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: struct gg_new_status80 p is declared on the stack at line 4 and is NEVER zero-initialised. Only p.status, p.flags and p.description_size are assigned before line 51 sends the ENTIRE struct with length sizeof(p). If gg_new_status80 contains ANY member beyond those three, or any inter-field/tail padding introduced by alignment, those bytes are transmitted verbatim from uninitialised stack memory to the remote GG server — an information-disclosure primitive (leak of stack contents, potentially pointers/secrets). The safe invariant is that either every byte of p up to sizeof(p) is assigned, or p is memset to 0 first, or the struct is __attribute__((packed)) with exactly the three assigned 32-bit fields and no other members. This must be confirmed against the struct definition (not shown); GG wire structs are usually packed with no padding, which is why this is a LATENT finding rather than a certain leak.

descr_len is a signed int but strlen() returns an unsigned size_t. On a 64-bit host a description whose length exceeds INT_MAX (~2 GB) truncates on assignment and can land on a NEGATIVE value. A negative descr_len is not caught by the guard `descr_len > GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE` (a negative is never greater than a positive ceiling), so the clamp is skipped. The negative value is then fixed into p.description_size (advertising a huge unsigned size to the server) and, more dangerously, passed as the length argument to gg_send_packet at line 52. If gg_send_packet interprets that length as an unsigned/size_t, the sign extension yields an enormous count and it reads far past the end of descr — an out-of-bounds read / crash and a further stack/heap disclosure. The impact requires a ~2 GB local description so it is low-likelihood, but the correct type for a byte length is size_t and the clamp should precede any signed handling.

On GG_PROTOCOL_110, descr_len is clamped to GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE and then incremented to MAXSIZE+1 at line 47 to append the terminating NUL. Reasoned through, this is memory-safe: the clamp only triggers when strlen(descr) >= MAXSIZE, so descr actually holds at least MAXSIZE+1 bytes (the char at index MAXSIZE plus its own terminator), and reading MAXSIZE+1 bytes stays in bounds. The real defects are (1) a CONTRACT mismatch — p.description_size reports MAXSIZE while MAXSIZE+1 payload bytes are sent, and the "+1 for the NUL" byte is in fact a live character (descr[MAXSIZE]), not a NUL, so the server receives an unterminated/over-length field; and (2) the invariant to uphold is that the byte that follows the clamped region is genuinely the string terminator, which only holds in the exact non-clamped case, so the two length notions must be kept consistent.

The description is clamped by BYTE count with no awareness of UTF-8 code-point boundaries, exactly as the XXX comment admits. When a description longer than GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE is truncated, the cut can fall in the middle of a multi-byte UTF-8 sequence, emitting an incomplete/invalid code point on the wire. This is a data-integrity / interop defect (malformed UTF-8 shown to peers, possible mis-decode) rather than a memory-safety bug; the invariant is that truncation must snap back to the last complete UTF-8 character boundary.

what_could_go_wrong.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The debug log formats descr with %s before descr is validated. If a caller passes descr as a non-NUL-terminated buffer, strlen inside the %s formatting over-reads; and if descr is NULL, %s on NULL is technically undefined behaviour (only conventionally rendered as "(null)"). Because the very next lines handle a NULL sess/descr, this trace runs on unvalidated inputs. Impact is limited (debug-only, local caller) but it is an over-read / potential NULL-deref surface. The invariant is that descr passed to libgadu must be a valid NUL-terminated string; the log should not assume more than the function itself has verified.

summary: Outbound libgadu status-serialisation routine. There is no remote-attacker heap-overflow here; the payload is locally supplied. The sharpest security concern is a LATENT stack information-disclosure: struct gg_new_status80 p is sent uninitialised via sizeof(p) after only three fields are set, so any extra member or padding leaks stack memory to the server (safe only if the struct is exactly-packed to the three assigned fields). Secondary issues are a signed-int description length that can go negative for >2 GB input (skipping the clamp and feeding a huge length to gg_send_packet) and the author-acknowledged non-boundary-aware UTF-8 truncation. Overall risk is low-to-medium and contingent on the (unshown) struct layout.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gg_change_status_descr(struct gg_session *sess, int status, const char *descr)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry signature; descr is caller-supplied text serialised to the network, status unvalidated, sess may be NULL.
Uninitialised on-wire header sent whole via sizeof(p) after only 3 fields assigned — latent stack info leak.
Owns the converted buffer; initialised NULL and freed unconditionally, so no leak/double-free on any path.
Signed length receiving a size_t from strlen; can go negative on >INT_MAX input and bypass the clamp.
Local capture of the send result; no security contract.
Logs descr before it is validated for NULL/termination — over-read / NULL-deref surface (debug-only).
Correct NULL guard for the session pointer.
Correct precondition gate; sends only on an established connection.
Scalar store of a caller value; no legal-value check but no memory hazard (later serialised only).
Transcodes to a heap buffer; NULL return handled with early -1 and no leak. Contract to confirm is that it returns a NUL-terminated buffer.
size_t->int truncation; combined with the clamp this is the sign-handling defect.
Clamp is skipped for negative descr_len and is byte-based (UTF-8 unaware); central to two findings.
Author-acknowledged mid-character truncation — data-integrity defect.
Safe empty-string substitution; sends a valid 0-length (or 1-byte NUL on proto110) description.
Correct endianness fixup of an assigned field.
Assigned field, later possibly overwritten; no hazard by itself.
Advertises the PRE-increment length; mismatches the transmitted byte count on proto110 and carries a negative descr_len if truncation occurred.
Post-clamp increment creates the size vs payload mismatch and the '+1 NUL' that is actually a live character in the clamped case.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: gg_send_packet(sess, GG_NEW_STATUS80, &p, sizeof(p), descr, descr_len, NULL) (lines 50-53)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Gather-write that transmits the uninitialised struct and reads descr_len bytes from descr; consumer of the sign and info-leak defects.
free(NULL)-safe and single free; correct cleanup.
State-machine transition on an offline status; no memory or input hazard.
Returns the send result; benign.

findings.title: Uninitialised on-wire header struct transmitted via sizeof(p) — stack information disclosure to the server
findings.anchor: struct gg_new_status80 p; sent with &p, sizeof(p) after only p.status/p.flags/p.description_size are set
findings.bug_class: Use of uninitialised stack memory / information exposure across a trust boundary
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0183_cwe310.c (this function) plus the struct gg_new_status80 definition (header not provided)
findings.invariant: Every byte of p from offset 0 to sizeof(p) that is placed on the wire must be explicitly initialised: either the struct is exactly-packed to the three assigned 32-bit fields with no other members and no padding, or p must be zero-initialised (memset/`= {0}`) before the field assignments.
findings.failure_mode: If gg_new_status80 has any additional member or alignment padding, those bytes retain prior stack contents and are sent to the remote GG server, leaking process memory (possible pointers/secrets) to a remote endpoint on every status change. Latent: depends on the unshown struct layout; GG wire structs are typically packed, in which case there is no leak.
findings.what_to_confirm: The full definition of struct gg_new_status80: confirm it is __attribute__((packed)) and contains exactly status, flags, description_size (12 bytes) with no other/reserved members and no padding; otherwise add p = memset/zero-init before use.

findings.title: Signed description length can go negative and bypass the clamp, feeding a huge size to gg_send_packet
findings.bug_class: Signed/unsigned length truncation (size_t stored into int) leading to out-of-bounds read
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0183_cwe310.c (this function); over-read materialises inside gg_send_packet (implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: A byte length must be held in an unsigned size_t, and the upper clamp must be applied before the value is used as a length; a negative or truncated length must never reach gg_send_packet or p.description_size.
findings.failure_mode: A description longer than INT_MAX truncates on the int assignment and can become negative; the `> GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE` guard does not catch a negative, so the clamp is skipped. The negative descr_len is fixed into p.description_size (huge advertised size) and passed to gg_send_packet, which, if it treats the length as unsigned, reads far past descr — OOB read/crash and further disclosure. Requires a ~2 GB local description, so low likelihood.
findings.what_to_confirm: The parameter type gg_send_packet uses for the fragment length (int vs size_t) and whether it validates for negatives; change descr_len to size_t and clamp before any use.

findings.title: Post-clamp descr_len++ makes description_size disagree with the transmitted byte count (proto 11.0)
findings.anchor: p.description_size = gg_fix32(descr_len) set before descr_len++ under GG_PROTOCOL_110
findings.bug_class: Length/contract inconsistency (declared size vs sent size)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0183_cwe310.c (this function) and the GG_PROTOCOL_110 framing spec
findings.invariant: The advertised description_size and the number of payload bytes actually written must be consistent with what the proto-11 receiver expects; when the length is clamped, the byte appended as a NUL terminator must genuinely be a terminator.
findings.failure_mode: On GG_PROTOCOL_110 the payload is descr_len+1 bytes while description_size still reports descr_len, and in the exact clamped case (strlen==MAXSIZE) the appended byte descr[MAXSIZE] is a real character, not a NUL, so the server sees an unterminated/over-length field. Memory-safe (the read stays in bounds), but a protocol-correctness defect that can desynchronise the receiver's parse.
findings.what_to_confirm: The GG_PROTOCOL_110 wire contract for GG_NEW_STATUS80: whether description_size is defined to exclude the trailing NUL, and whether description_size should be recomputed after the increment.

findings.title: Byte-count truncation splits multi-byte UTF-8 characters
findings.anchor: descr_len clamp to GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE with the XXX utf-8 note
findings.bug_class: Improper encoding/output — invalid UTF-8 on the wire
findings.invariant: Truncation of a UTF-8 string must snap to the last complete code-point boundary, never mid-sequence.
findings.failure_mode: A description longer than GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE is cut at a byte offset that may fall inside a multi-byte UTF-8 character (explicitly noted by the author's XXX comment), emitting a malformed final code point that peers may mis-render or reject. Data-integrity/interop only, not a memory-safety issue.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether callers ever pass descriptions exceeding the max size and whether the truncation should back off to a UTF-8 boundary.

findings.title: Debug log formats descr with %s before it is validated (NULL/non-terminated)
findings.code: gg_debug_session(sess, GG_DEBUG_FUNCTION, "** gg_change_status_descr(%p, %d, \"%s\");\n", sess, status, descr);
findings.bug_class: NULL dereference / out-of-bounds read via unchecked %s argument
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0183_cwe310.c (this function); actual formatting in gg_debug_session (implementation, file not provided)
findings.invariant: Arguments passed to %s must be valid NUL-terminated strings; the trace must not assume more validation than the function itself performs before line 11/30.
findings.failure_mode: Runs before the sess NULL-check and before descr is handled, so a NULL descr yields undefined behaviour (conventionally '(null)') and a non-NUL-terminated descr causes an over-read inside the formatter. Debug-only and locally-supplied, so impact is limited.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether gg_debug_session/its printf backend NULL-guards %s, and whether debug is compiled in for release builds.

audit_checklist: Dump the exact definition of struct gg_new_status80: confirm it is packed to exactly {uint32 status, uint32 flags, uint32 description_size} with no extra/reserved members and no padding; if not, zero-initialise p before the field stores to prevent leaking stack bytes to the server.
Change descr_len to size_t and apply the GG_STATUS_DESCR_MAXSIZE clamp before any use, so a >INT_MAX strlen cannot become negative and skip the clamp or reach gg_send_packet.
Verify the parameter type and validation gg_send_packet applies to each fragment length; ensure a negative/oversized length cannot cause an out-of-bounds read of descr.
Confirm the GG_PROTOCOL_110 contract for description_size (with vs without trailing NUL) and reconcile p.description_size with the descr_len++ so declared size matches transmitted bytes.
Confirm gg_encoding_convert returns a NUL-terminated buffer and cannot return a non-terminated result that would make the later strlen over-read.
Make the description truncation UTF-8 boundary-aware (per the XXX comment) so no partial multi-byte character is sent.
Ensure gg_debug_session/%s tolerates a NULL or non-terminated descr, or move the trace after input validation.
